title: A Paraphrase on the Seven Penitential Psalms, in English Verse, together with a Legendary Psalter of Saint Bernard
summary: The repository artifact copies University of Michigan CME item CME00017, an edition-level encoding of printed pp. 1-54 of William Henry Black's 1842 Percy Society volume. The selection contains a 124-stanza Middle English paraphrase based on British Library Sloane MS 1853 and collated with Harley MS 1704, Latin Appendix I from Harley MS 1845, and Middle English Appendix II from Royal MS 17 A XXVII. It excludes the preface, contents, and Black's printed notes on pp. 55-70. Six witnesses of the main paraphrase are recorded. A complete scan of the 1842 edition, a complete 406-canvas IIIF bundle for Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.20, two complete scholarly support sources, and one partial Sloane f. 3 crop are cached. No complete public facsimile was found for Sloane MS 1853, Harley MS 1704, Cambridge University Library MS Ff.2.38, Pepys MSS 1584 or 2030, Harley MS 1845, or a currently working public delivery of Royal MS 17 A XXVII.
review_notes: The cataloged unit is Black's three-part edited selection on pp. 1-54, not one organic medieval work, the complete 1842 volume, or any one manuscript.
The main poem's six witnesses are recorded as physical witnesses of A and B textual versions; Black directly transcribed Sloane MS 1853 and collated Harley MS 1704 only.
Black's 1414 Sloane attribution and Trinity R.3.20's 1413 attribution are later annotations and are not converted into a secure composition date.
The 1842 scan reads Royal MS 17 A XXVII for Appendix II. CME00017's XXVIII headnote conflicts with both the scan and current British Library catalog descriptions and is treated as an encoding error.
Witnesses, printed edition, page-image surrogates, digital encoding, repository artifact, and scholarly descriptions are modeled as distinct layers.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00017
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's conversion and publication workflow, supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID all identify the file as CME00017.
The headnote for Appendix II says Royal MS 17 A XXVIII, unlike the source edition's XXVII reading.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item CME00017
entities.description: A public digital text encoding Black's printed pp. 1-54, including stanza structure, Latin epigraphs and prayers, printed page breaks, and seven editorial notes, while omitting the volume's front matter and pp. 55-70 apparatus.

date_statements.label: repository XML structural review
date_statements.notes: The TEMPHEAD date documents review of this serialization, not necessarily first online publication.

notes: U-M applies No Copyright-United States to the item.
The encoding reproduces the erroneous XXVIII shelfmark in its Appendix II source headnote.

entities.label: Black, A Paraphrase on the Seven Penitential Psalms (1842)
alternate_labels: A Paraphrase on the Seven Penitential Psalms, in English Verse
entities.description: William Henry Black's Percy Society edition of the Sloane-based main paraphrase, collated against a Harley fragment, together with two manuscript-derived appendices and a separate notes apparatus.

bibliographic.citation: A Paraphrase on the Seven Penitential Psalms, in English Verse, supposed to have been written by Thomas Brampton, S.T.P., in the year 1414; together with a Legendary Psalter of Saint Bernard, in Latin and in English Verse, with notes by William Henry Black (London: printed for the Percy Society by T. Richards, 1842).
bibliographic.title: A Paraphrase on the Seven Penitential Psalms, in English Verse, supposed to have been written by Thomas Brampton, S.T.P., in the year 1414; together with a Legendary Psalter of Saint Bernard, in Latin and in English Verse

notes: CME00017 encodes printed pp. 1-54 only; it excludes the preface, contents, and notes on pp. 55-70.
The blank printed p. 48 is represented by a page-break marker before Appendix I.

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of Black's 1842 Percy Society edition
entities.description: A complete downloadable 92-page PDF scan of the 1842 volume.
entities.reproduction_format: Complete page-image scan with downloadable PDF

entities.label: British Library, Sloane MS 1853
entities.description: Black's base witness for the A version of the main poem, occupying ff. 3-30. The attribution to Thomas Brampton and date 1414 were added in a later, partly damaged hand and are not part of the original copied text.

date_statements.label: current British Library catalog date
date_statements.notes: Preserved alongside Black's early-fifteenth-century estimate and later scholarship's fifteenth-century dating.

date_statements.label: Black's manuscript dating
date_statements.notes: A historical editorial estimate, not substituted for the current catalog record.

notes: Black described a fair church-text hand and illuminated capitals; these historical observations await inspection against complete current images.

entities.label: PICRYL crop of the puzzle initial from Sloane MS 1853, f. 3
entities.description: One 1024-pixel JPEG crop showing the opening puzzle initial, not a complete leaf or manuscript surrogate.
notes: The provider labels the image as sourced from the British Library and public domain in the United States but cautions that metadata accuracy is not guaranteed.

entities.label: British Library, Harley MS 1704
entities.description: A composite manuscript whose paper section produced in the 1460s contains a fragment of the A version on ff. 13r-17v, corresponding to stanzas 62-116. Black collated it against Sloane MS 1853 and judged it later, inaccurate, corrupt, and linguistically modernized.

physical_description.support: Composite: parchment and paper; the relevant section is paper
notes: The relevant fragment is only a small part of a composite whose other sections range from the fourteenth to seventeenth centuries.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library, MS Ff.2.38
entities.description: A mid-fifteenth-century paper miscellany containing an imperfect B-version witness on ff. 28r-31v, beginning in stanza 56.

holding.notes: No exact current CUL digital catalog or complete public facsimile was established in this review.

entities.label: Trinity College Cambridge, MS R.3.20
entities.description: A fifteenth-century paper manuscript containing a complete B-version witness on pp. 197-232. A later addition attributes it to Thomas Brampton and dates it 1413.

physical_description.extent: 374 numbered pages, with covers, flyleaves, and stubs

entities.label: Complete Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.20 IIIF facsimile
entities.description: A 406-canvas color facsimile covering both covers, inside covers, flyleaves, stubs, all 374 numbered pages, and rear matter. The paraphrase occupies canvases 215-250, labeled pp. 197-232.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation manifest and JPEG page images

entities.label: Magdalene College Cambridge, Pepys MS 1584
entities.description: A fifteenth-century paper-and-parchment miscellany containing a complete B-version witness, titled 'The Seale of Mercy,' on ff. 28r-47v. The refrain was corrected throughout from 'Parce mihi domine' to 'Ne reminiscaris domine.'

entities.label: Magdalene College Cambridge, Pepys MS 2030
entities.description: A sixteenth-century paper miscellany containing an imperfect A-version witness on ff. 1r-17v. It begins at stanza 7, line 7, has a leaf lost after f. 7, ends imperfectly, and lacks stanzas 121-124.

entities.label: British Library, Harley MS 1845
entities.description: An imperfect Sarum Book of Hours containing the Latin Saint Bernard legend, eight psalm verses, and prayers transcribed by Black for Appendix I from ff. 15r-16v.

date_statements.label: manuscript
date_statements.value: Second or third quarter of the fifteenth century

notes: Produced in southern England, possibly London, for Sarum use.

entities.label: British Library, Royal MS 17 A XXVII
entities.description: A composite parchment codex whose first-quarter-fifteenth-century English and Latin prayer section on ff. 71r-97v includes the Middle English Saint Bernard legend and eight-verse psalter transcribed by Black from ff. 86v-88v for Appendix II.

date_statements.label: relevant manuscript section
date_statements.value: First quarter of the fifteenth century

notes: The earlier part belongs to the thirteenth-century Katherine Group; the Appendix II source lies in the distinct fifteenth-century section.
The 1842 scan reads XXVII. The XML's XXVIII reading is incompatible with the current catalog description of Royal MS 17 A XXVIII as a late-sixteenth-century 25-folio album.

entities.label: Sutherland, 'Performing the Penitential Psalms in the Middle Ages' (2010)
entities.description: A modern scholarly study of the main paraphrase's devotional and performative character, conventional Brampton attribution, 124-stanza structure, six-witness transmission, and A/B textual versions.

bibliographic.citation: Annie Sutherland, 'Performing the Penitential Psalms in the Middle Ages,' in Aspects of the Performative in Medieval Culture, ed. Manuele Gragnolati and Almut Suerbaum (Berlin and New York: de Gruyter, 2010), 15-37.
bibliographic.title: Performing the Penitential Psalms in the Middle Ages

entities.label: M. R. James, Bibliotheca Pepysiana: Mediaeval Manuscripts (1923)
entities.description: The printed catalog supplying detailed descriptions and contents for Pepys MSS 1584 and 2030.

bibliographic.citation: M. R. James, Bibliotheca Pepysiana: A Descriptive Catalogue of the Library of Samuel Pepys, part III, Mediaeval Manuscripts (London: Sidgwick and Jackson, 1923).
bibliographic.title: Bibliotheca Pepysiana: Mediaeval Manuscripts

scope.notes: The encoding excludes the preface, contents, and printed notes on pp. 55-70.

object.description: Main paraphrase in the base witness
Middle English legend and eight stanzas

scope.notes: The object shelfmark follows the 1842 scan and current catalog evidence, not the XML's erroneous XXVIII reading.

object.description: Puzzle initial at the opening of the poem

scope.notes: The crop does not reproduce the complete leaf or establish a complete manuscript facsimile.

object.description: Complete codex, including covers, endleaves, stubs, and numbered pages

scope.notes: Sutherland also compares the six-witness tradition and B version; the relation does not imply that her study describes Black's appendices.

rights.component: The local XML serialization and repository-specific changes
rights.jurisdiction: Repository terms and intended place of reuse
rights.basis: U-M applies No Copyright-United States to the upstream item, but the local XML supplies no express license governing this serialization or later repository changes.

rights.component: Materials presented in U-M CME item CME00017
rights.basis: The current U-M item page states that the materials are in the public domain and applies No Copyright-United States.
notes: No Copyright-United States is jurisdiction-specific and is not a worldwide license or dedication.

rights.component: The historical text and editorial material selected in CME00017
rights.basis: U-M presents the edition-derived selection as public-domain material under No Copyright-United States; the edition was published in 1842.

rights.component: Internet Archive page images and digital packaging
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms and intended place of reuse
rights.basis: The historical edition is public-domain in the United States, but no express item-level reuse license for the scan packaging was located in the reviewed item metadata.

rights.component: Trinity College Cambridge manuscript photography and IIIF delivery
rights.basis: The Trinity catalog and IIIF manifest apply Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 to the digital images.

rights.component: The specific derived JPEG crop delivered by PICRYL/GetArchive
rights.basis: The PICRYL landing page labels the selected image Public Domain and says it believes no known United States copyright restrictions apply, while cautioning that metadata accuracy is not guaranteed.
notes: The assertion is specific to this delivered crop and is not generalized to the physical manuscript, other British Library images, or other jurisdictions.

rights.component: The complete downloaded book PDF, including Sutherland's chapter
rights.basis: The current book PDF applies Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 International as of its 2018 open-access statement.
notes: The PDF also reproduces an older 3.0 statement in duplicated front matter; the later explicit 4.0 statement is recorded here without erasing that publishing-history irregularity.

rights.component: The 1923 catalog text and Internet Archive scan packaging
rights.basis: The catalog was published in 1923 and is public domain in the United States; no separate express license for the scan packaging was located.

rights.component: Any new manuscript photography supplied through British Library Digitisation Services; the same caution applies to requested Harley MS 1704, Harley MS 1845, and Royal MS 17 A XXVII images
rights.jurisdiction: British Library service terms and intended place of reuse
rights.basis: No images were supplied. The current service terms state that supplied material may involve intellectual-property rights and that copying or reproduction requires written permission.

rights.component: Any photography supplied by the Pepys Library; the same provider policy applies to Pepys MS 2030
rights.jurisdiction: Pepys Library provider terms and intended use
rights.basis: The Pepys Digital Library states that images are copyright the Pepys Library and that publication requires purchase and permission; no images of MSS 1584 or 2030 were supplied.

editorial_practices.source_feature: The complete Sloane codex and its main paraphrase on ff. 3-30
editorial_practices.representation: The 124-stanza main poem printed on pp. 1-47
editorial_practices.description: Black selected and transcribed the main paraphrase from Sloane MS 1853 rather than presenting the complete manuscript miscellany.

editorial_practices.representation: Harley variants and interventions documented principally in the notes on pp. 55-70
editorial_practices.description: Black collated the base Sloane text against the incomplete Harley witness, using the latter as a variant witness rather than a coequal base.
editorial_practices.scope: Main paraphrase stanzas 62-116 and printed notes

editorial_practices.source_feature: Sloane's Middle English spelling, thorn, yogh, word division, punctuation, damage, and later reingrossing
editorial_practices.representation: Th substituted for thorn; yogh retained; accents added for metrically significant final e; punctuation, brackets, supplies, and occasional emendations introduced and discussed in the apparatus
editorial_practices.description: Black calls the text scrupulous but also states explicit diplomatic and interpretive interventions. The edition must not be described as a photographic or purely diplomatic transcription.
editorial_practices.scope: Main text and appendices, with fuller explanations on pp. 55-70

editorial_practices.representation: Printed pp. 1-54 only, with seven retained footnotes
editorial_practices.description: The encoding selects the main text and two appendices while omitting the preface, contents, and pp. 55-70 notes that explain Harley variants, damaged text, supplies, and editorial choices.

evidence.citation: CME00017 XML source in the pinned CME submodule.
evidence.summary: The XML identifies the item and encodes the 124-stanza main poem, two appendices, printed page breaks, Latin quotations and prayers, Middle English verse, seven editorial notes, and the erroneous Royal MS 17 A XXVIII headnote.

evidence.citation: Deterministic structural inspection of CME00017.xml and alignment against Black's 1842 edition.
evidence.locator: BODY element counts and PB/@REF sequence
evidence.summary: The XML has three DIV1 units, 10 DIV2 subdivisions, 132 LG elements, 1,056 L elements, 14 P elements, 54 PB elements, 137 HEAD elements, and seven NOTE elements. Printed pp. 1-54 are represented, including an empty p. 48 before Appendix I.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, CME00017.
evidence.summary: The item page identifies Black, Brampton, the Percy Society source edition, the digital text, and public-domain/No Copyright-United States status.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, About the Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse.
evidence.summary: U-M describes collection texts as manually keyed from public-domain editions, proofed in house, encoded in basic TEI, and available in bulk XML.

evidence.citation: A Paraphrase on the Seven Penitential Psalms, in English Verse, ed. William Henry Black (London: Percy Society, 1842), Internet Archive item paraphraseonseve00bram.
evidence.locator: title page; preface, pp. vii-xiv; text, pp. 1-54; notes, pp. 55-70
evidence.summary: The complete scan confirms publication data, title, responsibilities, work boundary, source shelfmarks and folios, text, and the editorial apparatus omitted from CME00017.

evidence.citation: William Henry Black, preface and notes to A Paraphrase on the Seven Penitential Psalms (1842).
evidence.summary: Black identifies Sloane MS 1853 as base, explains the later Brampton/1414 attribution, records collation against Harley MS 1704, states transcription conventions and interventions, identifies both appendix sources, and says common authorship of Appendix II is uncertain.

evidence.citation: Folger Shakespeare Library, catalog record 59178, A Paraphrase on the Seven Penitential Psalms.
evidence.summary: The record confirms title, attributed author, editor, Percy Society publication, T. Richards as printer, 1842 date, xiv + one leaf + 70-page extent, and series placement.

evidence.citation: Annie Sutherland, 'Performing the Penitential Psalms in the Middle Ages,' in Aspects of the Performative in Medieval Culture (2010), 15-37.
evidence.summary: Sutherland describes the early-fifteenth-century, 124-stanza poem as devotional translation and commentary, treats Brampton as a conventional attribution, and distinguishes A and B textual versions across six witnesses.

evidence.citation: James R. Kreuzer, 'The Metrical Paraphrase of the Seven Penitential Psalms Traditionally Attributed to Thomas Brampton,' Traditio 7 (1951): 359-403.
evidence.summary: The accessible abstract/extract supplies the six-witness census and precise fragment boundaries; the complete article was paywalled and was not cached as a public downloadable source.

evidence.citation: British Library, Sloane MS 1853, Archives and Manuscripts record 040-002114208.
evidence.summary: The current record identifies the parchment manuscript, extent, sixteenth-century catalog dating, the paraphrase on ff. 3-30, onsite access, and no current digitized-content delivery.

evidence.citation: PICRYL, 'Puzzle initial from BL Sloane 1853 f. 3.'
evidence.locator: one crop from Sloane MS 1853, f. 3
evidence.summary: The landing page identifies a British Library Sloane f. 3 puzzle initial, labels the image Public Domain in the United States with a metadata caveat, and supplies only a derived crop rather than a full leaf or codex.

evidence.citation: British Library, Harley MS 1704, Archives and Manuscripts record 040-002047535.
evidence.summary: The record identifies the composite, its 1460s paper section, extent, and a legacy digitized-item URL that did not deliver when checked; Black and Kreuzer locate the paraphrase fragment on ff. 13r-17v.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, Cambridge University Library MS Ff.2.38.
evidence.summary: The descriptions identify the mid-fifteenth-century paper manuscript, size, extent, contents, and paraphrase on ff. 28r-31v; no complete public digital surrogate was located.

evidence.citation: Trinity College Cambridge, MS R.3.20 (James 600), online manuscript catalog.
evidence.summary: The catalog describes the fifteenth-century paper manuscript and complete paraphrase on pp. 197-232, including a later Brampton attribution dated 1413, and applies CC BY-NC 4.0 to the digital images.

evidence.citation: Trinity College Cambridge, IIIF Presentation manifest for MS R.3.20.
evidence.locator: 406 canvases; work at canvases 215-250, labels pp. 197-232
evidence.summary: The manifest supplies a complete ordered facsimile including covers, endleaves, stubs, all numbered pages, image services, and a CC BY-NC 4.0 rights statement. A normalized complete 406-image bundle is cached.

evidence.citation: Visual and manifest-label inspection of the complete Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.20 IIIF bundle.
evidence.locator: canvases 215-250 of 406, zero-based indexes 214-249, labels pp. 197-232
evidence.summary: Inspection confirms the work range and complete object coverage represented in the cached bundle; image delivery was normalized to 1800-pixel width without reducing canvas coverage.

evidence.citation: M. R. James, Bibliotheca Pepysiana: Mediaeval Manuscripts (London, 1923).
evidence.summary: James describes the physical manuscripts, their contents, work folios, incipits, completeness, and the refrain correction in Pepys 1584.

evidence.citation: Magdalene College Cambridge, Pepys Library visitor and research information.
evidence.summary: The library is closed to general visitors during refurbishment until 2027, while researchers may enquire in advance and a photographic service is available.

evidence.citation: Magdalene College Cambridge, Pepys Digital Library.
evidence.summary: The page enumerates a selected group of digitized manuscripts that excludes Pepys 1584 and 2030, claims copyright in its images, and requires purchase and permission for publication.

evidence.citation: British Library, Harley MS 1845, Archives and Manuscripts record 040-002047676.
evidence.summary: The record identifies the imperfect Sarum Book of Hours, parchment support, 130-folio extent, southern English or London production in 1425-1474, and no current public Digitised Content; Black locates Appendix I at ff. 15r-16v.

evidence.citation: British Library, Royal MS 17 A XXVII and its fifteenth-century component, records 040-002107258 and 041-003475038.
evidence.summary: The records identify the composite parchment codex and its English and Latin prayer material on ff. 71r-97v from the first quarter of the fifteenth century. Historical catalog data reports full digital coverage, but no current delivery was found.

evidence.citation: British Library, Royal MS 17 A XXVIII, Archives and Manuscripts record 040-002107259.
evidence.summary: The current record describes a 25-folio late-sixteenth-century calligraphic album, proving incompatible with the fifteenth-century Appendix II source read as XXVII in the 1842 scan.

evidence.citation: British Library, Digitisation Services.
evidence.summary: The service accepts shelfmark-and-folio requests subject to preservation and feasibility review, charges fees, photographs manuscript originals, and distinguishes supply from permission to reproduce.

evidence.citation: British Library, Imaging Services terms and conditions, updated January 2026.
evidence.summary: The terms warn that supplied material may involve intellectual-property rights and require written permission before copying or reproducing supplied material.

open_questions.question: Who composed the main paraphrase, and what weight should be given to the later Brampton attributions dated 1413 and 1414?
next_steps: Inspect the attribution hands in complete images of Sloane MS 1853 and Trinity R.3.20.
Review post-Sutherland scholarship on Brampton's identity, the six-witness stemma, and A/B version chronology.
Keep author, annotator, and composition-date claims separate unless stronger evidence emerges.

open_questions.question: How should Sloane MS 1853's physical date and textual layers be reconciled across Black's early-fifteenth-century description, later scholarship's fifteenth-century dating, and the current British Library catalog's sixteenth-century date?
next_steps: Obtain complete color images and a current curatorial assessment of support, script, decoration, reingrossed portions, and the attribution hand.
Consult modern codicological and palaeographical scholarship before assigning a single witness date.

open_questions.question: Can complete, reusable digital facsimiles be obtained for the seven physical manuscripts lacking a currently cached complete public surrogate?
next_steps: Ask the British Library whether historical complete imaging of Royal MS 17 A XXVII and Harley MS 1704 can be restored or supplied.
Request complete-object imaging for Sloane MS 1853, Harley MSS 1704 and 1845, and Royal MS 17 A XXVII, including covers, endleaves, blanks, and adjacent works.
Ask Cambridge University Library about existing full imaging or imaging of Ff.2.38.
Ask the Pepys Library about complete photography of MSS 1584 and 2030 and secure written reuse terms before caching supplied files.
notes: The Trinity R.3.20 bundle is complete; the PICRYL Sloane crop is explicitly partial.

open_questions.question: What item-specific production record identifies U-M's exact keying copy and explains its selection of pp. 1-54, omission of the apparatus, and XXVIII shelfmark error?
next_steps: Ask U-M Library for item-level keying specifications, source-copy identification, proofreading history, and correction logs.
Propose correcting the Appendix II source headnote to Royal MS 17 A XXVII in the upstream CME encoding while preserving the documented source reading history.

open_questions.question: Is Appendix II's Middle English Legendary Psalter authorially or textually related to the main paraphrase beyond Black's editorial juxtaposition?
next_steps: Compare dialect, meter, vocabulary, and transmission against all main-poem witnesses and other versions of the Saint Bernard eight-verses legend.
Retain separate work-part modeling unless evidence supports a closer relationship.
